sentences.text: My name is Leopold-- original name is Leopold Pfefferberg.
I was born on March the 20th, 1913, in Cracow Podgorze, the most beautiful town in in the whole Poland, old over 1,000 years old.
I was born to a beautiful, middle class Jewish family, religious.
My grandparents were very religious.
My parents were religious too.
Not orthodox, but conservative.
I have a beautiful youth.
I was the first in the family of my grandfather's family, where there was 11 brother and sister.
I was the oldest grandson, and I was like a treasure for my grandfather.
Three years later, my sister was born on February 9, 1916.
This time, my father was not present during the birth of my sister.
He was already drafted to the Austrian Army during the First World War.
And we didn't know in this time where he was located.
We found a few months later, after my sister was born, that he was wounded in the western-- northwestern part of Poland.
My father-- first, my father's name was David Pfefferberg.

My father was a representative of company from Germany in this particular time, has a [INAUDIBLE] office in city of Cracow, and represent [INAUDIBLE] company was importing goods from South America, like coffee, tea, and nuts, and raisins, and other types of normal imports from this part of the world and was doing very well.
But the war started in 1914, and he was drafted.
And he got wounded in 1916, and then he was released.
My father-- The family on my father was a very well-known Jewish family of city of Cracow for centuries.
His family was consistent about nine sister and brother.
He was the oldest one.

When he finish 18-- when he was 18 years of age and finished high school, he went to America in 1905 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the family has relatives, cousins, and uncles someplace in New York.
I never found them.
Lately, I'm getting now some feeding back that they are from the family of Cracow Pfefferberg.
My father spent seven years in America.
Their family was wealthy here in America, but they give him a chance only to stay with them about a month, and then they said, you are on your own.
He got a job in Metropolitan Opera in New York, in the chorus.
He has a beautiful tenor voice, and he was singing over there.
[LAUGHS]
He-- he came back because as the oldest son-- his father got gravely ill in the 1912, and my father returned to support the family.
And my grandfather-- I never knew my grandfather from my father's side because I was born in 1913.
He died 1912, but I was-- my name, Leopold, is after my grandfather from my father's side.
My sister was born in 1916, and her name was after the grandmother from my mother's side.
And their name was Pauline.
My-- when my father-- I don't know exactly, because still was a very young man in his 20s.
He came back, and he met by coincident his future wife, who also was from very prominent Jewish family, middle class family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from family name Goldfluss.
Moses Goldfluss was my grandfather.
All very well situated.
They got married, and I was born in a short time, about exactly nine or ten months after [LAUGHS] they got married.
Because I was-- they married in 1912, and I was born in in 1913, in March.
Yes.
My father.
My father, when he come from the war in the '20s, you must remember, when he was wounded, he was released.
But in 1920, the Bolshevik invaded Poland, so he was called to the Polish Army.
You understand?
So the whole period of time since 1914 'til 1921, he was mostly on and off from the army.
He couldn't make any living, but my mother was very talented interior decorator, so she was supporting the family.
In the beginning, we were living together in my grandfather-- Moses Goldfluss, my mother's father and and mother-- we were living in the beginning in their house.
They have a big farm not far away from city of Cracow in Podgorze.
And later on, when she started making a good living, we take apartment in-- in Podgorze, and its main street of Podgorze, street Podgorze.
There was not only in [NON-ENGLISH].
The Cracow has, in this particular period of time, close to about 60,000 but grew later on to about 75- 80,000 population before the war.
Oh, there quite [? many synagogues ?]  there.
Main was the-- the temple, what that exists even today.
But the rest of them, small shuls.
You know, dependent of your wealth and of your status in the community, you belonged to certain shuls, what have the similar group of Jews, prominent type of Jews.
And my father belonged to the [INAUDIBLE] shul, to the [INAUDIBLE].
We were-- I was going to the shul 'til I became 18 years of age.
[LAUGHS] And when I graduated from high school and went to the university, I went on the shul to the big holidays I-- on account of the sentiment and account of this.
And my father was still a conservative Jew.
You understand?
I went to the shul.
I finished high--
You see, you know, let me tell you something about my father.
He didn't have a university degree, but he has a high school degree.
You see?
But he was extremely talented, spoke fluently few languages; Polish, German, English, Hebrew, Yiddish, and in writing and speaking.
And he wrote beautifully.
My father was very talented in writing.
I don't have the talents of my--
No.
I was one of the lucky one who had went to the government high school.
This meant free of paying, free of charge.
I went to the high school in city of Podgorze, very prominent high school.
And it was difficult.
It's not like here, high school here.
Gymnasium was a much, much higher level because the classes-- when I was-- I was 10 years old when I went to the first class of gymnasium.
We have already three
German, Latin, and Greek.
[LAUGHS]
Coincidentally, it was substantial amount.
In the class was roughly between 35 and 40 students, was about 50% was a Jewish student.
We didn't have no problem in the-- in the high school with anything.
Like, again, the Jews and things.
The only problem was
to get to the gymnasium, you have to be at B plus student minimum.
When, for example, general public, the Catholic student could get the minute they are entitled to go, they were accepted.
We have to come- go-- everybody has to go through the entry examination, but was much more difficult to pass for the Jewish boy, the demand was much bigger, because they have to eliminate it.
According of the status of Poland-- because in Poland, an average was between 3 and a-- 3 and a half million Jewish population before the-- between first and second war.
They quotas.
There was quotas for the school-- 10%.
Was easier to get to a a government school for the Jewish boy because we are excelling in-- in studying.
We knew to-- to stay in the school, you have to be very good.
University, the quota was very strict, particular to the things like medical school and the Department of Physical Education school.
Was easier to get to law or to other department with different qualification.
Engineering was very difficult to get in.
I went to medical school.
[INAUDIBLE].
The medical school with a special implementation to the physical education, but this was all more like preparing this young people to a teaching in a high school, in gymnasium, or university.
When you became a master degree or doctor degree, you could go and teach on the university.
I got only up to master degree, and I-- and I have master degree in education from University of Krakow.
And I became a teacher in 1934.
The minute I finished high school, I already got a job [INTERPOSING VOICES] because there were no Jewish--
No.
In high school.
Even the first year I was in a Polish high school.
Because when they send me for the practice on the first year, I was assigned to the high school which I four years before graduate.
And all of my ex-teachers became my colleagues, and was little bit strange, but I build a pretty good reputation over there.
No.
With my sister, was a little bit difficult.
She couldn't get to the government school because they were not too many girl high school.
You remember that in Poland in this time was not co-education, not co-ed.
So there were mens/boys gymnasium and girl.
And the amount of boys gymnasium was three times as many as a girl.
It was difficult to get for my sister to there.
You had-- you didn't have other brothers or sisters?
No.
My sister went to the Hebrew gymnasium.
[INAUDIBLE] extremely hard high school and a very high scholastic level.
You have to be-- because they have to-- to graduate high school-- in this time, the high school was eight years long.
Later on, the division came between junior high and [INAUDIBLE].

You have to be a very good student to pass the examination of all the general classes, what every high school has to go through, plus also Hebrew classes, where they have extra curriculum in Hebrew in language and religion and so much--
This- only I was a member of Jewish school or university, but not-- they were not political organization.
They were mostly more like a fun and game and meeting people and have good time.
School-- high-- there was high school-- how do we call this?
There was Jewish high school organization.
Yeah.
But there was some groups of Jewish organization, like Zionists.
Oh, no.
You see-- no, I didn't belong to any political type of group, not to the Zionist, not to Betar, not to any of them.
I belonged to the sports organization, [? my ?] [INAUDIBLE].
It was very well organized sport organization because I was a-- a-- I would say, extraordinary sportsman in every field.
I was swimmer, and I was skier.
Skier and swimmer and hockey player and everything.
Yeah, I was excellent.
And I became first Jew in Polish National Ski Association that reached the rank of the instructor, a full fledged instructor of teaching skiing.
But for me, as a teacher in physical education was very important, because I could make extra money by teaching children or anybody else.
Oh, I stayed with my family.
I never--
I stayed at home.
I have a wonderful family, warm relationship with my parents and with my sister, and we were an extremely loving family.
My parents protected and sheltered us and tried to do everything possible for the children, and--
Is a--  is interesting question what you are asking me to answer.
In my youth-- in my youth, I never saw any [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anti-Semitic against me personally.
There were some story, what you hear, that something happened, sometime maybe over exaggerate.
Because the anti-Semitism in Poland, what we consider was the biggest country with the biggest anti-Semitism, is a very over-- over exaggerate.
Poland, in general terms, was not more or less anti-Semitic than in Germany or France.
I even consider the Frenchman and Englishman and German more anti-Semitic inclined because they do this in the white gloves.
And a Polack, when he didn't like you, he told you, you dirty Jews, and this was everything over.
Oh, this I-- I watched the history of Hitler from the beginning, from about 1928, when he becames to take little power and was lot of story written in the papers and things like this.

And we knew that this man is really a danger to society, not only in the Germany, but for influence also every other country around him, like France, Czechoslovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Croatia, Yugoslavia, Hungary, Poland.
I tell you [? quickly ?] you see, I was-- remember, I was a teacher already in 1934, that I went to the army, to the officer school in 1935, and then I got my master degree in 1936.
That I was more involved with the personal things in this particular time than thinking about the political aspect what is going on.
But then when I start to teach in a Jewish gymnasiums-- because that's what I--
You see, in this time, I realized that what is going, and we watched much more carefully what was going in Germany.
Particularly in 1937, 1938, they start to expel German citizen born in Poland.
And they were shifting in-- shipping them from all over Europe, Czechoslovakia.
For example, in 1938, my mother, my father, accepted a family from Moravia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we kept the whole family 'til they decide to go out because the Czechoslovakia has agreement with-- with Russia in 1938 that they can go through Russia.
And they were sent and permitted by Germans to go over there after that.
I never-- I never heard that a Jew became in a-- in an officer's school in the Polish army.
All this--
Well, I was the lucky one.
[LAUGHS]
You see, I'll tell you what happen.
When I was on the university-- because when you became high school graduate, you are eligible to go to officer school.
Every graduate, regardless of your religion or nationality or what it is, you are a Polish citizen.
You have to go to the army.
But you can get exempt when you start to study.
So I got the exemption for five years because takes about five years to graduate university get the degree.
You see?
I finish in in three and a half years.
Put it this way, and I figured out what the-- I want to go to there.
I personally want to go to the army.
I want to prove that we are not any different, the Jews, than any other Polish Catholic boys.
And they send me to the most strictest, the most difficult officer school to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was [INAUDIBLE] city of Germans because [NON-ENGLISH] was annexed to Germany 'til 1918.
In 1919, they got the part of the Germany to Poland-- originally was always for century Polish ground.
And in the [NON-ENGLISH] was lots of German family who became Polish citizen.
And in this particular officer school-- this was close to about 400 students in the [INAUDIBLE], 90% were the Germans.
We called them Wasserpolak.
I was the only one Jew in the officer school, and-- and-- I'll tell you something.
There were certain requirement for the Jews.
You are the best, or you're out.
When you are not the best, you going to the army for two years.
Or you want to finish in one year, you have to be best.
And I was very lucky.
I was-- I finished with the highest rank from the officer school, as a sergeant officer candidate.
And three of us was there.
You saw the picture.
The two boys who are friends of mine, and this was suddenly the picture, we were the three top in the class.
Yes.
I have right away a job.
From all over the Poland, the all Jewish private school was looking for a teacher of physical education.
Also, I have a right to teach ROTC as a officer candidate because I was nominated in 1938 for full-fledged officer.
And I was the guy what was important.
I have offer from every high school in Poland, but I stay first in Cracow.
And I will tell you right now.
In 1938, I got a wonderful job in the brand new Jewish high school in Czestochowa.
Magnificent school, with the best teachers what the money can buy, what they selected.
And they can only lure them there to the little town from city of Cracow or Warsaw, or or [NON-ENGLISH] or [?
Wilno ?] by giving a almost double salary.
I was-- [LAUGHS] you must understand, I mean, this time I was 24-- no.
In this time, I was 26 years old, and I was getting close to 300 American US dollar a month.
I don't think so anybody in America in 1937 or '38 got so much money.
I took the job, and over there, till the 1939, I was-- the last two year, '38, '39, I was teaching there.
And there was beautiful two years for me as a young man.
Then I came back to Cracow when the war started.
I was teaching in city of Krakow in the Hebrew gymnasium, one the best and most prominent high school in Poland, Jewish high school in Poland.
When the war coming, I was-- I was-- mobilization came to me with a secret telephone call because I was train as a company commander.
You see?
And the same day, in 19-- and I was 1930, I got the order to go immediately to my unit, what was in [NON-ENGLISH], was 100, 200 kilometers away from Cracow, in east, toward east.
And I arrive there Friday morning, at about 4 o'clock in the morning. 6 o'clock, the war started, and the first planes was bombing-- bombing all the barracks and the-- the the regiment.
Even [NON-ENGLISH] was bombed the first Friday night.
On the third of-- I became second in command of the company, because the professional captain-- I was a new lieutenant-- captain was the officer in charge.
I was his right hand, the second in command.
When we went on the [? sixth, ?] we have the first [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] attack by the German motorized army by the city of [NON-ENGLISH].
And this what happened, that our company commander was killed, and I took over the company.
And we stayed for two days defending this particular [INAUDIBLE].
They were about 400 meters away, and I was shooting, and I was-- I didn't see that my bullet to shoot anybody.
One things I know that my company was completely almost wiped out. 240 soldiers.
I took out from under fire about 45.
That's all there is.
All got killed or wounded or-- or run away.
Who knows what happened.
I was wounded day after.
Yes.
No.
There was about seven-- seven of them.
And then you went to a German [INAUDIBLE].
No.
No.
No.
I was still limping and still-- our company was first towards the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] invaders.
And when we start to retreat, the whole division, my company was the last one protecting the retreat.
So we were going toward the [? wolf. ?] Toward east.
And by the city of-- on River [NON-ENGLISH], we dig in, and we decide to stand up against the Germans.

And when we were sitting there, I remember like today, this was around this 20th or 22nd of September, two tanks come from the west side, and about three tanks from the east side, the German tanks and the Russian tanks.
And we start standing up.
We are thinking the war will be over because the Russians are coming to help us.
But then I saw a strange thing.
There were a [? lip ?] on the river [NON-ENGLISH], a bridge.
The officer-- the German officer got out from the tank.
Russian officer got out from his tank.
They went on the bridge, they shook hands, they come back, and we knew that we were-- we were double crossed over there.
And the Germans crossed the River [NON-ENGLISH] and took [INAUDIBLE].
And we were forced to retreat towards the wharf.
But my orderly-- 18 years old boy from city of [NON-ENGLISH], from a village not far away from [NON-ENGLISH], was very badly wounded in the arm.
So I decide to take him back to the hospital in [NON-ENGLISH], even that I knew that the [NON-ENGLISH] will be, in a few hours, in the hands of Germany.
But other way, he would not survive.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I brought him over there to the hospital.
And the same night in the hospital, I met my friend who is a priest, young fellow who was a priest, was from [NON-ENGLISH].
He came to me.
He said to me, [NON-ENGLISH] when this [NON-ENGLISH], is the name of the orderly-- will not amputate his arm.
He will not live in the morning, and he doesn't want to let us do it.
The doctor can do nothing.
I, as a priest, cannot-- this boy.
The orderly was a Catholic boy.
Maybe you can do something.
So I limped to his bed, and I said, you have to let them do it.
Other way, you'll die.
Oh no.
I, as a farmer, I-- without the hands, I am not worth anything.
I said, look.
You're losing the arm for fighting for-- for Poland.
Poland will never forget you.
When you give me order to amputate the arm, I will obey.
I give him the order.
The hand was amputated.

The boy survived, and strange things-- I got a-- I have it here, a letter from a Polish nurse from Poland that-- because they found out I put an ad in the paper that I'm looking for my orderly-- that he lives someplace around the [NON-ENGLISH], that I should contact the [? competent ?] organization of Poland.
They will find him for me.
I didn't have the time, but this came about a month ago.
So I have still time to look it for him.
So he was one of the very rare Jews that was fighting the Germans before--
There were lots of Jews fighting the Germans in Polish Army, quite a bit.
Soldiers and officers.
There were other Jewish officers, doctors, and-- and-- trained professional, even professional Jewish officer were there.
My life was completely changed because I was destined to do something else.
But on account of the war, my whole education, my whole talent was wasted-- [INAUDIBLE].
On the-- on the end of the war?
I-- I was taken prisoner of war because I was in city of [NON-ENGLISH], and the German-- because remember, we are under jurisdiction of the army, not the Gestapo or SS.
The army control.

They give us a choice to the officer-- they didn't ask that I'm Jewish or not Jewish-- that we can go to the prisoner of war to Russia because they were-- Russia were taking back this part of the river [NON-ENGLISH] from Germany.
So they give a choice only to the officer, not to unlisted man, that we can go to prisoner of war to Russia or the prisoner of a war to Germany.
We were officially prisoner of war of German, but they give us a choice.
Many of my colleagues, because we are thinking to go to east to Romania, across Romania, go to Yugoslavia.
From Yugoslavia, take the ship to France, and-- and the Polish army was organized in-- in French in this time.
Well, I was thinking different way.
I knew the Russia never signed the Geneva Convention.
And I didn't-- I knew mentality what will happen, the Stalinist period.
You understand?
That I will probably wind out someplace in Siberia.
This was what I was thinking.
The German is signed the-- the Geneva Convention.
I said, they will obey the rules.
It doesn't meant they will, but this was my thinking in this time.
Then I decide to go to German prisoner of war instead to Russian.
And I found out later-- and I have the book to prove it-- that the 16,000 Polish officers, and some of them the Jews were there, was killed in [NON-ENGLISH], in surrounding area.
I escape.
The-- the train was passing from city of Cracow, and we have to change the train to different one.
And when I was there, it's interesting how easy, little story can change everything.

When we were there in the hospital-- because I have a three and a half years medical study-- the professor of anatomy from Warsaw University, Professor [NON-ENGLISH], was the officer in charge of the hospital.
That because he was a doctor.
And he needed an assistant, and I was his assistant.
And I was with the nurses and with the other doctor doing operation, very serious some of them.
You understand?
When I will talk about this, you wouldn't believe it, what we were doing there.
You see?
And
